title: Burr Hole Craniotomies Through EEG
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"cgrvr","text":"This protocol describes the surgical procedures, instrumentation, and reagents necessary to make burr hole craniotomies (0.5-1 mm in diameter) in a mouse that has already been implanted with an EEG Array and Headframe. This surgery is necessary to perform simultaneous recordings of EEG and Neuropixels from a head-fixed mouse in-vivo.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"fjcm7","text":"Only perform this procedure in accordance with IACUC and veterinary requirements.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"7t74a","text":"This procedure is a follow-up to the \"EEG Array and Headframe Implant Surgery\" protocol. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"aeokq","text":"Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) should be used at all times while operating this protocol.\n","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a7vl7","text":"Isoflurane Warning: Acute over-exposure to waste anesthetic gases (WAG) may cause eye irritation, headache, nausea, drowsiness or dizziness. Repeated exposure may cause damage to cardiovascular system and central nervous system. Refer to MSDS for additional information.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Follow procedures to prepare surgical space as per steps 1-3.5 of the "General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery" protocol linked below. General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery by Avalon Amaya
Disinfect the cement by wiping with an alcohol wipe. Repeat 3 times.
Attach the dovetail clamp and bregma stylus. Zero the stereotaxic coordinates over bregma using the mark created during the initial EEG and Headframe Implant Surgery.
Check all the coordinates that were marked in the previous surgery in relation to bregma.

Carefully cut and remove a square of the polyimide of the EEG array, ensuring that you do not cut any of the electrodes or wires on the array. The sides of the square should be ~1-1.5 mm long. Once you remove the polyimide, the skull should be visible. Now you should be able to see the black sharpie mark on the skull from when you marked the craniotomy coordinates during the EEG implant surgery.

Use an FG 1/4 drill bit and carefully drill in a circular motion to thin a circle on the skull that is ~0.5-1.5 mm in diameter. Do not apply pressure to the surface of the skull when drilling. Ensure that you are drilling consistently so that the entire area of skull is thinning at the same rate. Use forceps to test the thickness of the bone and stop when the surface gives slightly at the touch of the forceps. As you are drilling, keep the surface clear of debris to maintain visibility. Use a dust cleaner to blow debris off or apply a drop of ACSF to the area and wipe it away with a sterile Kimwipe TM /cotton swab/sugi swab.
Add a plastic ACSF reservoir on top of the cement and adhere along the edges (inside and outside) with dental cement.

Use an EF4 drill bit to drill around the circumference of the circle to continue thinning the skull. Continue drilling until a crack forms in the thinned skull and/or cerebrospinal fluid leaks through the thinned surface. Be careful to stop drilling immediately when this happens and inspect the skull. If the crack only occurs in one area, continue drilling along the circumference until there is a crack along the majority of the circumference.

Use a drop of ACSF to wash off any debris from drilling and to hydrate the underlying tissue. Using forceps, extract the bone fragments from the tissue, being careful not to damage the underlying tissue. Use a piece of Hemostatic Agent Surgifoam or ACSF covered by Kwik-Cast to ensure the tissue stays hydrated while you finish the surgery.
go to step #9 and repeat drilling for all craniotomies.
Once you have drilled all the craniotomies, wash the entire area with ACSF and remove any Surgifoam, as this can hinder insertion of Neuropixels probes.
Add a drop of ACSF over each of the craniotomies and carefully cover any exposed brain tissue with Kwik-Cast.

Once the cement has dried, fill the reservoir with ACSF and make sure that it is not leaking. If it is leaking, patch the holes with dental cement. Remove ACSF from the reservoir using a cotton swab or similar.
Administer up to 1.0 mL of Lactated Ringer's Solution via subcutaneous injection.
Obtain the postoperative weight of the mouse.
Allow the mouse to recover in the home cage placed on a 38 °C heating pad.
Administer post-operative injections as per IACUC protocol. Wait 24 hours before performing experiments.
Attach ear bar headframe clamp to the stereotaxic frame.

Follow procedures to anesthetize the mouse as per steps 25-27.5 in "General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery" protocol. Skip step 27.2. Instead, use the titanium headframe previously implanted on the mouse to secure mouse in the ear bar headframe clamp. Do not use ear bars or bite bar.
Follow procedures to prepare the mouse for surgery as per steps 28-30 in "General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery" protocol.

Use a scalpel blade to carefully slice and remove the Kwik-Cast layer under the hole you drilled. Be very careful not to damage the underlying EEG array when removing the Kwik-Cast. If there is an electrode underneath where you removed the Kwik-Cast, you may need to widen the hole you created in the cement and remove additional Kwik-Cast, to avoid cutting the electrode or the connecting wires. This will result in a slight shift in the craniotomy coordinates.

Use an FG 4 drill bit and manually drill a small hole (~1-2 mm in diameter) in the cement at all of the marked craniotomy locations. Once the Kwik-Cast is visible, stop drilling. Schematic depicting the different layers that must be drilled/cut through in order to access the cortex.
Follow steps 36-46 as per the "General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery" protocol.
Turn off the isoflurane. Gently remove the mouse from the headframe clamp and nose cone.
creator: Lydia C. Marks
